theory.question: Explain how to build a key-value store on top of an append-only file using an in-memory hash map. Describe how lookups work and explain the two structural limitations of this design.
theory.answer:
Headline: an in-memory hash map of `key -> byte offset` over an append-only log file. Bitcask (the default storage engine in Riak) does exactly this. It is genuinely fast, but it only works when (a) all keys fit in RAM and (b) you never need range queries.

"The simplest possible indexing strategy is this: keep an in-memory hash map where every key is mapped to a byte offset in the data file—the location at which the value can be found."

How it actually works:
 - Write: append `key,value` to the data file, update the hash map with the new offset.
 - Read: hash-lookup in RAM → seek to the offset on disk → read the value. If the data is in the OS page cache, the read is satisfied with zero disk I/O.
 - Values can be larger than RAM (only the index has to fit); the workload that fits best is 'lots of writes per key, not too many distinct keys' — think a `play_count` counter keyed by video URL.

The two structural limitations that matter (these are the deal-breakers when someone proposes this design):
 1. "The hash table must fit in memory." Putting the hash map on disk is theoretically possible but in practice 'requires a lot of random access I/O, it is expensive to grow when it becomes full, and hash collisions require fiddly logic.' So your key space is bounded by RAM.
 2. "Range queries are not efficient. For example, you cannot easily scan over all keys between kitty00000 and kitty99999—you'd have to look up each key individually in the hash maps." The hash map has no order, so every range scan is N point lookups.

Follow-up:
 - 'How would you scale beyond one machine?' Shard by hash of key (consistent hashing), keep per-shard hash + log; that's how Riak does it.
 - 'How do you free disk space?' Compaction over segment files — that's the next question.
 - 'When would you actually choose this over an LSM-tree or B-tree?' Bounded key set, write-heavy, point lookups only — counters, sessions, caches with persistence.

Comment: Redis is essentially this design taken to the limit (everything in RAM, optional AOF persistence) — and the production rule 'never run `KEYS *` against Redis' is just the no-range-scan limitation showing up in practice.

theory.question: Your append-only data file grows without bound as users overwrite the same keys. Explain compaction and segment merging and why an append-only design beats overwriting values in place.
theory.answer:
Headline: rotate the log into fixed-size segments, and run a background compactor that rewrites each segment keeping only the latest value for each key. Append-only beats in-place because sequential writes are an order of magnitude faster than random ones, crash recovery is trivial (you never see a half-written value), and merging keeps the on-disk footprint from drifting.

"A good solution is to break the log into segments of a certain size by closing a segment file when it reaches a certain size, and making subsequent writes to a new segment file. We can then perform compaction on these segments... Compaction means throwing away duplicate keys in the log, and keeping only the most recent update for each key."

How this plays out in practice:
 - The compactor runs in a background thread on frozen, immutable segments.
 - Segments are never modified after they have been written, so the merged output is written to a new file. Once it is durably written, reads switch to the new merged segment and the old ones are deleted.
 - Several segments can be merged in one pass, which is essential because compaction usually shrinks segments meaningfully.

Why 'just overwrite the old value in place?' is the wrong answer. Kleppmann gives three reasons:
 1. "Appending and segment merging are sequential write operations, which are generally much faster than random writes, especially on magnetic spinning-disk hard drives. To some extent sequential writes are also preferable on flash-based solid state drives (SSDs)."
 2. "Concurrency and crash recovery are much simpler if segment files are append-only or immutable. For example, you don't have to worry about the case where a crash happened while a value was being overwritten, leaving you with a file containing part of the old and part of the new value spliced together."
 3. "Merging old segments avoids the problem of data files getting fragmented over time."

Follow-up:
 - 'Doesn't compaction kill performance?' It competes for disk bandwidth with foreground writes — the standard mitigation is rate-limiting compaction and monitoring backlog (RocksDB calls it 'pending compaction bytes').
 - 'What if compaction can't keep up?' Segment count grows, reads slow down because they have to check more files, eventually disk fills. You need explicit monitoring.
 - 'How is this different from a log-structured filesystem?' Same principle (turn random writes into sequential), one level up.

Comment: Kafka exposes both modes — `log.cleanup.policy=delete` (time-based retention) and `log.cleanup.policy=compact` (keeps only the latest value per key, same idea). Postgres's `pg_repack` extension rebuilds a fragmented heap by copying live rows into a fresh file in the background — same trick again.

theory.question: Name the practical issues that a production hash-indexed log must solve beyond the textbook 'append + hash map' version.
theory.answer:
Headline: the toy version is missing five things — a real binary record format, soft delete, snapshot-based crash recovery, partial-write detection, and a concurrency model. These five together turn the homework version into something that doesn't lose data in production.

"Lots of detail goes into making this simple idea work in practice. Briefly, some of the issues that are important in a real implementation are":

1. File format. "CSV is not the best format for a log. It's faster and simpler to use a binary format that first encodes the length of a string in bytes, followed by the raw string (without need for escaping)." Length-prefixed records remove parsing ambiguity, avoid escape rules, and let you skip cleanly over a record without parsing its contents.

2. Deleting records. "If you want to delete a key and its associated value, you have to append a special deletion record to the data file (sometimes called a tombstone). When log segments are merged, the tombstone tells the merging process to discard any previous values for the deleted key." You cannot delete in place — the file is append-only — so deletes are themselves writes.

3. Crash recovery. "If the database is restarted, the in-memory hash maps are lost." In principle you can rebuild them by scanning every segment from the start, "but that might take a long time if the segment files are large, which would make server restarts painful. Bitcask speeds up recovery by storing a snapshot of each segment's hash map on disk, which can be loaded into memory more quickly." The takeaway: startup latency is a first-class concern; pure scan-on-startup is unacceptable past a few GB.

4. Partially written records. "The database may crash at any time, including halfway through appending a record to the log. Bitcask files include checksums, allowing such corrupted parts of the log to be detected and ignored." Without checksums, you can read garbage and not know it.

5. Concurrency control. "As writes are appended to the log in a strictly sequential order, a common implementation choice is to have only one writer thread. Data file segments are append-only and otherwise immutable, so they can be read concurrently by multiple threads." One writer, many readers — no mutex on the segments themselves, no torn writes, no MVCC complexity for the storage layer.

Follow-up:
 - 'Why one writer thread instead of many?' All writes touch the same file; multi-writer adds locking that erodes the sequential-write advantage. One writer + group commit is the standard pattern.
 - 'How big should a segment be?' Trade off: larger segments mean less merging overhead and fewer hash maps; smaller segments mean faster compaction and shorter recovery. A few hundred MB is typical.
 - 'What goes in the on-disk hash map snapshot?' Key → (offset, value size, timestamp) — enough to do a read directly without re-scanning.

Comment: Kafka's broker log uses every one of these tricks — binary length-prefixed records, periodic index files (the equivalent of Bitcask's hash-map snapshot), CRC32C per batch, single-threaded log appender per partition.

theory.question: Describe the layout of a Sorted String Table (SSTable) and three concrete advantages it has over a hash-indexed log.
theory.answer:
Headline: an SSTable is a segment file where the key-value pairs are sorted by key and each key appears at most once. That single invariant unlocks merge-sort-style compaction, a sparse in-memory index (so you don't need to keep every key in RAM), and block-level compression. It also gives you cheap range scans for free.

"We call this format Sorted String Table, or SSTable for short. We also require that each key only appears once within each merged segment file (the compaction process already ensures that)."

Three concrete wins:

1. Efficient merging, even when files are bigger than memory. "The approach is like the one used in the mergesort algorithm... you start reading the input files side by side, look at the first key in each file, copy the lowest key (according to the sort order) to the output file, and repeat." When the same key appears in two input segments, the value from the newer segment wins. Crucially, this is a sequential read on each input and a sequential write on the output — no random I/O.

2. Sparse in-memory index. "In order to find a particular key in the file, you no longer need to keep an index of all the keys in memory." If you know the offsets for the keys 'handbag' and 'handsome', then 'handiwork' must lie between them, so you can binary-search a sparse index and scan the few KB in between. "You still need an in-memory index to tell you the offsets for some of the keys, but it can be sparse: one key for every few kilobytes of segment file is sufficient." This solves the 'must fit all keys in RAM' problem from the previous question.

3. Block compression. "Since read requests need to scan over several key-value pairs in the requested range anyway, it is possible to group those records into a block and compress it before writing it to disk... Besides saving disk space, compression also reduces the I/O bandwidth use." Each sparse-index entry points at the start of a compressed block.

Bonus: range queries (`WHERE key BETWEEN ... AND ...`) become a single sequential scan instead of N point lookups — the exact thing hash indexes couldn't do.

Follow-up:
 - 'How do you build a sorted file when writes arrive unsorted?' Memtable + WAL — that's the next question.
 - 'How dense should the sparse index be?' Trade-off between index size and bytes scanned per lookup. LevelDB uses one entry per ~16 KB block by default.
 - 'How does an SSTable handle deletes?' Same tombstone trick as before — a deletion marker key that wins over any older value until compaction drops both.

Comment: Cassandra's `*.db` files and RocksDB's `*.sst` files are SSTables, and each one ships with a small 'index block' (the sparse index Kleppmann describes) and a 'filter block' (Bloom filter, covered later).

theory.question: Writes arrive in any order, but SSTable files must be sorted by key. Describe how a memtable using a red-black or AVL tree solves this, including the crash-recovery path.
theory.answer:
Headline: keep writes in an in-memory self-balancing BST (red-black or AVL tree) so they are always sorted, flush it to disk as an SSTable when it gets big enough, and use a separate write-ahead log to recover the memtable after a crash.

"Maintaining a sorted structure on disk is possible (see 'B-Trees'), but maintaining it in memory is much easier. There are plenty of well-known tree data structures that you can use, such as red-black trees or AVL trees. With these data structures, you can insert keys in any order and read them back in sorted order."

The three trees Kleppmann mentions — what they are and why an engineer should care:

1. AVL tree (Adelson-Velsky and Landis, 1962). A binary search tree where, for every node, the heights of the left and right subtrees differ by at most 1. After every insert or delete, the tree may rotate (a constant-time local pointer rewiring) until the invariant holds again. The strict balancing keeps height very close to log₂(n), so lookups are as fast as a BST can be, but writes do more rotations on average.
   Shape after inserting 1..7 in order: a tree of height 3 with 4 as root, 2 and 6 as children, etc. — no degeneration into a linked list.
   Use when: read-heavy in-memory index, no concurrent writers.

2. Red-black tree (Bayer, 1972; Guibas & Sedgewick, 1978). Also a BST, but each node carries a single bit of color (red or black) and the tree maintains four invariants: the root is black, no red node has a red child, every root-to-leaf path passes through the same number of black nodes, and null leaves are black. These invariants are looser than AVL's, so the tree height is bounded by 2·log₂(n+1) — a bit taller — but rebalancing requires fewer rotations per write. 
   Use when: balanced read/write mix; this is the default ordered map in most standard libraries (`std::map` in C++, `TreeMap` in Java, `BTreeMap` is the exception in Rust — see below).

3. B-tree (Bayer & McCreight, 1970). A multi-way generalization: every internal node holds up to k sorted keys and up to k+1 child pointers. When a node overflows, it splits and pushes the median up to the parent — propagating up only when the parent itself overflows, which is what keeps the tree balanced. The branching factor k is chosen so a node fits in one block of the storage medium (a CPU cache line for in-memory B-trees, a 4-16 KB disk page for on-disk ones).
   Why it dominates on disk: a B-tree of fan-out 500 reaches 256 TB in 4 levels, so a key lookup is at most 4 page reads. A binary tree (red-black/AVL) over the same data would be ~31 levels deep — 31 random disk seeks per lookup, which is unusable.

How these map to the memtable choice: writes hit the in-memory tree, where the gap between AVL, red-black, and a skip list is at most a few cycles per operation. Either of the binary trees gives you O(log n) insert + in-order traversal, which is all the flush algorithm needs. "When a write comes in, add it to an in-memory balanced tree data structure (for example, a red-black tree). This in-memory tree is sometimes called a memtable." When the memtable grows past a threshold (typically a few MB), the engine walks the tree in-order, streams the entries to a new SSTable file (sequential write, no extra sort), and starts a fresh memtable for incoming writes.

Why not a B-tree for the memtable: B-trees pay off when the cost of moving a node into the working set (a disk page or a cache line) dominates the cost of comparisons inside the node. In RAM that's barely true — binary trees have simpler insertion logic, and a skip list (used by RocksDB and Redis sorted sets) is even easier to make lock-free for concurrent writers. The book reserves B-trees for the on-disk side because that's where their wide-fan-out advantage matters.

Reads scan in order of recency: memtable first, then the newest on-disk SSTable, then progressively older ones.

The crash-safety gap and how to close it: "If the database crashes, the most recent writes (which are in the memtable but not yet written out to disk) are lost. In order to avoid that problem, we can keep a separate log on disk to which every write is immediately appended... Its only purpose is to restore the memtable after a crash. Every time the memtable is written out to an SSTable, the corresponding log can be discarded."

So a complete write is: append to WAL on disk → insert into memtable in RAM → ack the client. The WAL is not sorted and never read in steady state; it's purely insurance.

Follow-up:
 - 'AVL or red-black, which would you pick?' Red-black for the standard library / mixed workload, AVL when reads dominate. In practice an LSM memtable uses a skip list — same asymptotics, much simpler lock-free implementation.
 - 'Why not a B-tree for the memtable?' In RAM, comparisons are cheap relative to pointer chases; the wide-fan-out advantage of a B-tree only shows up when a node fetch is expensive (disk page or cache line).
 - 'What happens if the memtable flush fails halfway through?' The flush writes to a new file; only after `fsync` does the engine switch the WAL pointer. Half-flushed files are discarded on recovery.
 - 'How do you avoid blocking writes during a flush?' Double-buffered memtables — the new one accepts writes while the old one is being serialized.

Comment: RocksDB and LevelDB both use a skip list memtable by default and expose the WAL as `WAL_ttl_seconds`. Cassandra also uses a skip list (`memtable_allocation_type=offheap_objects`) and calls the WAL the commitlog. The Linux kernel uses red-black trees inside the CFS scheduler and in `mm/rmap.c`; `std::map` and Java's `TreeMap` are red-black; PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB use B+ trees on disk.

theory.question: Describe the full read and write path of an LSM-tree and explain why Bloom filters and different compaction strategies are added in real implementations.
theory.answer:
Headline: an LSM-tree is memtable + WAL on the write path, and memtable → newest SSTable → older SSTables on the read path, with background compaction stitching things together. Bloom filters short-circuit reads for keys that don't exist; size-tiered vs leveled compaction trade write amplification against read fan-out.

"Originally this indexing structure was described by Patrick O'Neil et al. under the name Log-Structured Merge-Tree (or LSM-Tree)... Storage engines that are based on this principle of merging and compacting sorted files are often called LSM storage engines."

Write path (in one breath): append to WAL → insert into the in-memory memtable (red-black tree / skip list) → ack. When the memtable hits a threshold, flush it as the newest on-disk SSTable, drop the corresponding WAL segment.

Read path: "First try to find the key in the memtable, then in the most recent on-disk segment, then in the next-older segment, etc." Background compaction merges SSTables to reduce read fan-out and to discard tombstones and obsolete versions.

The Bloom filter optimization (this is the canonical 'name an LSM optimization' answer): "The LSM-tree algorithm can be slow when looking up keys that do not exist in the database: you have to check the memtable, then the segments all the way back to the oldest (possibly having to read from disk for each one) before you can be sure that the key does not exist. In order to optimize this kind of access, storage engines often use additional Bloom filters. (A Bloom filter is a memory-efficient data structure for approximating the contents of a set. It can tell you if a key does not appear in the database, and thus saves many unnecessary disk reads for nonexistent keys.)" Bloom filters have false positives but no false negatives, so 'definitely not in this SSTable' is a hard answer and lets the engine skip the file entirely.

Compaction strategies — name both: "The most common options are size-tiered and leveled compaction." Size-tiered merges newer/smaller SSTables into older/larger ones (Cassandra default, HBase). Leveled compaction splits the keyspace into smaller SSTables across levels, where each level is roughly 10× the previous (LevelDB, RocksDB). Leveled gives lower read fan-out and more predictable reads but higher write amplification; size-tiered is cheaper on writes but worse on reads and on space.

Follow-up:
 - 'When does an LSM-tree fall over?' When compaction can't keep up with writes — segments pile up, reads slow down, eventually disk fills. The fix is rate-limiting writes or scaling out.
 - 'Why is the Bloom filter sized per SSTable, not global?' Each SSTable is immutable, so its membership set is fixed and the filter never needs to support deletes.
 - 'What about range queries?' SSTables are sorted, so a range scan reads each level in parallel and merges — but compaction artifacts and tombstones can hurt latency.

Comment: in RocksDB `bloom_filter` and `compaction_style` are the exact knobs that map onto this paragraph. ScyllaDB defaults to size-tiered; CockroachDB and TiDB sit on top of RocksDB with leveled compaction.

theory.question: Describe how a B-tree stores data, define branching factor, and explain how the algorithm keeps the tree balanced as keys are inserted.
theory.answer:
Headline: a B-tree is a sorted on-disk tree of fixed-size pages (usually 4 KB), where each interior page has hundreds of child pointers and the tree splits pages on insert to stay balanced. A 4-level B-tree with branching factor 500 indexes 256 TB — which is why almost every relational database uses one.

"Like SSTables, B-trees keep key-value pairs sorted by key, which allows efficient key-value lookups and range queries. But that's where the similarity ends: B-trees have a very different design philosophy."

The layout: "B-trees break the database down into fixed-size blocks or pages, traditionally 4 KB in size (sometimes bigger), and read or write one page at a time. This design corresponds more closely to the underlying hardware, as disks are also arranged in fixed-size blocks." Each page has an on-disk address, which interior pages use as pointers to children. One designated page is the root.

Looking up a key: start at the root, which holds keys and child references. "Each child is responsible for a continuous range of keys, and the keys between the references indicate where the boundaries between those ranges lie." Descend through interior pages until you hit a leaf, which holds the value inline (clustered) or a pointer to the value in a heap file.

Branching factor: "The number of references to child pages in one page of the B-tree is called the branching factor... in practice... typically it is several hundred." High branching factor → flat tree → few disk seeks per lookup.

How it stays balanced: to insert, find the leaf whose range contains the new key and write it. "If there isn't enough free space in the page to accommodate the new key, it is split into two half-full pages, and the parent page is updated to account for the new subdivision of key ranges." Splits propagate up only when the parent itself overflows. "This algorithm ensures that the tree remains balanced: a B-tree with n keys always has a depth of O(log n). Most databases can fit into a B-tree that is three or four levels deep, so you don't need to follow many page references to find the page you are looking for. (A four-level tree of 4 KB pages with a branching factor of 500 can store up to 256 TB.)"

Follow-up:
 - 'Why fixed-size pages?' They match the disk sector / SSD page boundary, so one read = one page = one I/O. Modern engines often run on 8 KB or 16 KB pages.
 - 'How is delete different from insert?' Deleting from a leaf is easy; rebalancing (page merge) when leaves get half-empty is more involved — that's why most engines defer it.
 - 'B-tree vs B+ tree?' B+ tree stores values only in leaves, keeps interior pages narrow (more children per page), and links leaves left-right for fast range scans. Almost every 'B-tree' in a real database is a B+ tree.

Comment: PostgreSQL's nbtree, MySQL InnoDB's clustered primary index, SQL Server's clustered indexes, Oracle's standard indexes — all B+ trees. The 256 TB number is why a single-machine relational database can plausibly hold a company's entire transactional dataset.

theory.question: B-tree updates overwrite pages in place. Describe what can go wrong on a crash, and how WAL, copy-on-write, and latches protect against it.
theory.answer:
Headline: a B-tree update can touch multiple pages (split + parent rewrite), and a crash mid-operation corrupts the index. Two production solutions: a write-ahead log that records each modification before it's applied, or copy-on-write (LMDB) that writes new pages to fresh locations and atomically swaps the root pointer. Latches handle in-memory concurrency, separately from user-visible row locks.

"The basic underlying write operation of a B-tree is to overwrite a page on disk with new data... This is in stark contrast to log-structured indexes such as LSM-trees, which only append to files (and eventually delete obsolete files) but never modify files in place."

What can corrupt on crash: "If you split a page because an insertion caused it to be overfull, you need to write the two pages that were split, and also overwrite their parent page to update the references to the two child pages. This is a dangerous operation, because if the database crashes after only some of the pages have been written, you end up with a corrupted index (e.g., there may be an orphan page that is not a child of any parent)."

Solution 1 — Write-Ahead Log (the standard relational answer): "It is common for B-tree implementations to include an additional data structure on disk: a write-ahead log (WAL, also known as a redo log). This is an append-only file to which every B-tree modification must be written before it can be applied to the pages of the tree itself. When the database comes back up after a crash, this log is used to restore the B-tree back to a consistent state." The WAL turns a 'multi-page update' into one durable append + an eventually-consistent set of page writes that can be redone from the log.

Solution 2 — Copy-on-write (LMDB): "A modified page is written to a different location, and a new version of the parent pages in the tree is created, pointing at the new location." The old pages remain valid until a single atomic root-pointer swap commits the new version. No WAL needed for crash recovery; you also get cheap snapshot isolation for free, because old roots can be kept around as read snapshots.

Concurrency angle — latches: "Multiple threads are going to access the B-tree at the same time — otherwise a thread may see the tree in an inconsistent state. This is typically done by protecting the tree's data structures with latches (lightweight locks)." Important distinction: latches are short-lived, per-page, in-memory mutexes; row locks are user-visible, transactional, and can be long-lived. LSM-trees mostly avoid latches on the on-disk part because SSTables are immutable.

Follow-up:
 - 'What is a torn write?' A page write that gets partially applied (e.g., 4 KB write across two physical sectors); PostgreSQL handles it by writing full-page images to WAL the first time a page is dirtied after a checkpoint (`full_page_writes=on`).
 - 'When is copy-on-write better?' Heavy snapshot workloads, embedded use, when you want no WAL — that's the LMDB pitch.
 - 'Why does InnoDB do a double-write buffer?' Same torn-write protection but at the storage engine layer instead of at the WAL layer.

Comment: PostgreSQL WAL + checkpointing implements ARIES-style recovery; LMDB-derived stores (BoltDB, MDBX) use pure CoW; SQLite added WAL mode in 2010 and that's why concurrent readers stopped blocking writers.

theory.question: Define write amplification, explain why both B-trees and LSM-trees suffer it, and list two B-tree optimizations.
theory.answer:
Headline: write amplification is the ratio of bytes the storage engine writes to disk vs bytes the application sent it. Both architectures pay it — B-trees from WAL + full-page writes + splits, LSM-trees from compaction. It matters because SSDs have finite erase cycles and because in write-heavy systems disk bandwidth is the throughput ceiling.

For B-trees: "A B-tree index must write every piece of data at least twice: once to the write-ahead log, and once to the tree page itself (and perhaps again as pages are split). There is also overhead from having to write an entire page at a time, even if only a few bytes in that page changed. Some storage engines even overwrite the same page twice in order to avoid ending up with a partially updated page in the event of a power failure."

For LSM-trees: "Log-structured indexes also rewrite data multiple times due to repeated compaction and merging of SSTables. This effect—one write to the database resulting in multiple writes to the disk over the course of the database's lifetime—is known as write amplification."

Why it matters:
 1. SSDs have a finite number of P/E cycles per block — 10× write amplification means 10× faster wear-out. Enterprise SSDs are rated in DWPD (drive writes per day) and write amplification eats directly into that budget.
 2. "In write-heavy applications, the performance bottleneck might be the rate at which the database can write to disk... the more that a storage engine writes to disk, the fewer writes per second it can handle within the available disk bandwidth." If your engine has WA=20, a 1 GB/s SSD only gives you 50 MB/s of logical writes.

Two B-tree optimizations to name (pick from the book's list):
 1. Copy-on-write instead of WAL (LMDB). Modified pages are written to a new location; the parent is rewritten to point there. "This approach is also useful for concurrency control." Trade: garbage collection becomes a real concern.
 2. Abbreviated keys in interior pages (B+ tree). "Especially in pages on the interior of the tree, keys only need to provide enough information to act as boundaries between key ranges. Packing more keys into a page allows the tree to have a higher branching factor, and thus fewer levels."
 3. Sequential leaf layout + sibling pointers. Range scans walk leaf siblings without going back to the parent; engines try to keep leaves physically contiguous so a range scan is sequential.

Follow-up:
 - 'Which architecture has lower write amplification in practice?' Depends entirely on workload. Leveled LSM compaction can hit 10-30× WA; a B-tree can be 2-5× if rows are small relative to the page. Benchmark before claiming.
 - 'How do SSDs hide this from you?' The flash translation layer (FTL) does its own log-structured rewriting internally, so the OS-level write count understates the on-flash count. Tools like `smartctl` expose 'Total LBAs Written' and 'NAND Writes' to compare.
 - 'How to measure it?' RocksDB exposes `rocksdb.compact.write.bytes / rocksdb.bytes.written`. For Postgres, compare WAL bytes (`pg_stat_wal.wal_bytes`) to logical bytes written by clients.

Comment: this is exactly the metric that drove Facebook to invest in RocksDB tuning — at their scale, halving write amplification means halving the SSD fleet for write-heavy workloads.

theory.question: Compare LSM-trees and B-trees in terms of write throughput, read latency, tail latency, fragmentation, and transactional locking.
theory.answer:
Headline: LSM-trees win on write throughput, compression, and SSD wear; B-trees win on predictable read latency, single-place-per-key semantics, and transactional locking. Pick LSM for write-heavy or append-mostly workloads (logs, time series, messaging); pick B-tree when reads dominate and you need strict transactional isolation. "There is no quick and easy rule for determining which type of storage engine is better for your use case, so it is worth testing empirically."

"As a rule of thumb, LSM-trees are typically faster for writes, whereas B-trees are thought to be faster for reads. Reads are typically slower on LSM-trees because they have to check several different data structures and SSTables at different stages of compaction."

LSM-tree advantages (the 'why pick Cassandra over Postgres' case):
 1. Write throughput. "LSM-trees are typically able to sustain higher write throughput than B-trees, partly because they sometimes have lower write amplification (although this depends on the storage engine configuration and workload), and partly because they sequentially write compact SSTable files rather than having to overwrite several pages in the tree. This difference is particularly important on magnetic hard drives, where sequential writes are much faster than random writes."
 2. Compression and storage. "B-tree storage engines leave some disk space unused due to fragmentation: when a page is split or when a row cannot fit into an existing page, some space in a page remains unused. Since LSM-trees are not page-oriented and periodically rewrite SSTables to remove fragmentation, they have lower storage overheads, especially when using leveled compaction." 30-50% smaller on disk is common.

LSM-tree downsides:
 1. Tail latency. "At higher percentiles the response time of queries to log-structured storage engines can sometimes be quite high, and B-trees can be more predictable." Compaction shares disk bandwidth with foreground I/O, so p99 spikes are an ops reality.
 2. Compaction backlog risk. "If write throughput is high and compaction is not configured carefully, it can happen that compaction cannot keep up with the rate of incoming writes. In this case, the number of unmerged segments on disk keeps growing until you run out of disk space, and reads also slow down because they need to check more segment files. Typically, SSTable-based storage engines do not throttle the rate of incoming writes, even if compaction cannot keep up, so you need explicit monitoring to detect this situation."
 3. Transactional locking. "An advantage of B-trees is that each key exists in exactly one place in the index, whereas a log-structured storage engine may have multiple copies of the same key in different segments. This aspect makes B-trees attractive in databases that want to offer strong transactional semantics: in many relational databases, transaction isolation is implemented using locks on ranges of keys, and in a B-tree index, those locks can be directly attached to the tree."

Follow-up:
 - 'Which one do you pick for a metrics store?' LSM — InfluxDB, Prometheus's TSDB, M3DB are all LSM-style. Write-mostly, append-mostly, time-ordered keys.
 - 'Why does Discord use ScyllaDB instead of Postgres for messages?' Write-heavy fan-out + needs single-digit-ms reads at scale + no multi-row transactions needed. Same reason Cassandra was the prior choice.
 - 'Why does every fintech still use Postgres?' Predictable latency + strict serializable isolation via row locks attached to the B-tree.

Comment: see the RUM Conjecture (Athanassoulis et al.) — read, update, and memory overhead are a three-way trade-off; you can be good at two but not all three. B-trees pick read+memory; LSM-trees pick update+memory; main-memory stores pick read+update.

theory.question: How to build a secondary index on a non-unique column? Explain the two ways to handle non-unique keys and the tradeoff between heap-file, clustered, and covering indexes.
theory.answer:
Headline: a secondary index on a non-unique column either stores a postings list per key, or appends a row-id suffix to each key. Where the row data actually lives is a second decision: heap file (one copy of the row, indexes point in), clustered index (row inlined in the index — InnoDB primary), or covering index (some columns inlined). Each step trades read speed for write cost and storage.

"A secondary index can easily be constructed from a key-value index. The main difference is that keys are not unique; i.e., there might be many rows (documents, vertices) with the same key."

Two ways to handle non-uniqueness (both work on B-trees and LSM-trees):
 1. "Making each value in the index a list of matching row identifiers (like a postings list in a full-text index)." One key → many row IDs.
 2. "Making each key unique by appending a row identifier to it." Then the index is functionally unique but rows with the same logical key still cluster together.

Three storage choices — the canonical trade-off:

Heap file (PostgreSQL default). "The place where rows are stored is known as a heap file, and it stores data in no particular order... The heap file approach is common because it avoids duplicating data when multiple secondary indexes are present: each index just references a location in the heap file, and the actual data is kept in one place." Update-in-place works if the new value fits in the old slot. If it doesn't, the row moves and either every index is updated to the new location, or a forwarding pointer is left at the old location.

Clustered index (MySQL InnoDB primary, SQL Server option). "It can be desirable to store the indexed row directly within an index. This is known as a clustered index. For example, in MySQL's InnoDB storage engine, the primary key of a table is always a clustered index, and secondary indexes refer to the primary key (rather than a heap file location)." Primary-key lookups are one I/O; secondary-index lookups are two (secondary → PK → row). Choice of primary key shape suddenly matters a lot — random UUIDs cause page splits, monotonic IDs are cheap.

Covering index. "A compromise between a clustered index (storing all row data within the index) and a nonclustered index (storing only references to the data within the index) is known as a covering index or index with included columns, which stores some of a table's columns within the index. This allows some queries to be answered by using the index alone (in which case, the index is said to cover the query)."

The tradeoff is consistent: "As with any kind of duplication of data, clustered and covering indexes can speed up reads, but they require additional storage and can add overhead on writes. Databases also need to go to additional effort to enforce transactional guarantees, because applications should not see inconsistencies due to the duplication."

Follow-up:
 - 'When would you NOT add a covering index?' Write-heavy column, or many secondary indexes already (multiplies the storage and write cost).
 - 'Why does InnoDB make secondary index reads slower?' They store PK references, so a covering query is great but a non-covering one is two B-tree walks.
 - 'How does an LSM handle non-unique secondary indexes?' Same two options. Cassandra stores `<index_value, primary_key>` as the SSTable key — appending the PK to make it unique.

Comment: in PostgreSQL the covering-index syntax is `CREATE INDEX ... INCLUDE (col1, col2)`; in MySQL InnoDB you 'just get' a covering index when the SELECT list + WHERE columns all appear in some secondary index. Always check with `EXPLAIN` — 'Using index' (MySQL) or 'Index Only Scan' (Postgres) is the marker.

theory.question: Describe how full-text search and fuzzy indexes work, including the data structure Lucene uses for typo-tolerant search.
theory.answer:
Headline: a full-text index is an inverted index — `term -> postings list of document IDs` — stored in LSM-like immutable sorted segments. For typo tolerance, Lucene uses a finite-state automaton over the terms, then converts it on the fly into a Levenshtein automaton that emits every term within edit-distance k.

"A full-text index is much more complex than a key-value index but is based on a similar idea: given a word in a search query, find all the documents (web pages, product descriptions, etc.) that mention the word. This is implemented with a key-value structure where the key is a word (a term) and the value is the list of IDs of all the documents that contain the word (the postings list). In Lucene, this mapping from term to postings list is kept in SSTable-like sorted files, which are merged in the background as needed."

So at the storage layer, Lucene is just an LSM-tree with terms as keys. New documents go into an in-memory segment; the segment flushes to disk as an immutable inverted index; background merges combine segments. That's why `_refresh` in Elasticsearch is cheap (just makes the latest in-memory segment searchable) but `_forcemerge` is expensive (rewrites everything).

Beyond exact matches, real-world search engines layer on:
 1. Synonyms — 'car' also matches documents with 'automobile'.
 2. Stemming / lemmatization — 'running' matches 'run'.
 3. Phrase and proximity queries — words near each other in the same document.
 4. Fuzzy search for typos. "To cope with typos in documents or queries, Lucene is able to search text for words within a certain edit distance (an edit distance of 1 means that one letter has been added, removed, or replaced)."

The fuzzy data structure: "In LevelDB, this in-memory index is a sparse collection of some of the keys, but in Lucene, the in-memory index is a finite state automaton over the characters in the keys, similar to a trie. This automaton can be transformed into a Levenshtein automaton, which supports efficient search for words within a given edit distance." The practical effect: a fuzzy search runs in time proportional to the number of matching terms, not the size of the term dictionary — so `"jhon"~1` does not scan a billion documents looking for 'john'.

Follow-up:
 - 'Why use Elasticsearch instead of `WHERE body LIKE '%foo%'`?' LIKE with a leading wildcard cannot use a B-tree (the index is sorted left-to-right). Even with a trigram index, ranking and tokenization features aren't there.
 - 'How does relevance ranking work?' BM25 / TF-IDF — score = function of term frequency in doc and inverse document frequency across the corpus. Each posting in the postings list stores positions and term frequency.
 - 'What about vector search?' Modern Lucene (and Elasticsearch / OpenSearch) added HNSW graphs alongside the inverted index, for k-NN over embeddings. Same engine, different index structure.

Comment: PostgreSQL approximates this with a GIN index over a `tsvector` column — same idea (postings lists), simpler tokenizer, lower ceiling. For true full-text + scale + relevance ranking, run a dedicated engine.

theory.question: Compare OLTP and OLAP across read pattern, write pattern, dataset size, and bottleneck. Why does running both on one engine usually fail?
theory.answer:
Headline: OLTP is small reads + small writes keyed by primary key, latency-sensitive, seek-bound; OLAP is huge scans over a few columns of many rows, throughput-bound, bandwidth-limited. A single engine struggles because (a) analytical queries blow out the OLTP buffer cache and lock contention spikes, and (b) the index structures that win for OLTP (B-tree on a primary key) are useless for full-table aggregates.

"The difference between OLTP and OLAP is not always clear-cut, but some typical characteristics are listed in Table 3-1."

| Property         | OLTP                                       | OLAP                                       |
| Read pattern     | Small number of records per query, by key  | Aggregate over a large number of records   |
| Write pattern    | Random-access, low-latency, user input     | Bulk import (ETL) or event stream          |
| Primary user     | End user / customer via web app            | Internal analyst, for decision support     |
| Data represents  | Latest state of data (current point in time)| History of events over time               |
| Dataset size     | GB to TB                                    | TB to PB                                   |
| Bottleneck       | Disk seek time                              | Disk bandwidth                             |

"OLTP systems are typically user-facing, which means that they may see a huge volume of requests. In order to handle the load, applications usually only touch a small number of records in each query. The application requests records using some kind of key, and the storage engine uses an index to find the data for the requested key. Disk seek time is often the bottleneck here."

"Data warehouses and similar analytic systems are less well known, because they are primarily used by business analysts, not by end users. They handle a much lower volume of queries than OLTP systems, but each query is typically very demanding, requiring many millions of records to be scanned in a short time. Disk bandwidth (not seek time) is often the bottleneck here, and column-oriented storage is an increasingly popular solution for this kind of workload."

Why you separate the two systems:
 1. "Database administrators... are usually reluctant to let business analysts run ad hoc analytic queries on an OLTP database, since those queries are often expensive, scanning large parts of the dataset, which can harm the performance of concurrently executing transactions."
 2. The indexing structures that win for OLTP (B-tree / LSM key-value) accelerate point lookups but force a full-row read for aggregates. If a 100-column fact table only needs 3 columns for a report, row-store reads 100 × more data than necessary.

The standard architecture: "A data warehouse, by contrast, is a separate database that analysts can query to their hearts' content, without affecting OLTP operations." Data is ETL'd from one or more OLTP systems into a star schema — central fact table (one row per event) + dimension tables (who/what/where/when/how/why).

Follow-up:
 - 'What if the analytics use case is small?' One Postgres instance + read replica + materialized views is often enough until ~100 GB.
 - 'How fresh does the warehouse need to be?' If hourly is enough → daily ETL. If sub-minute → CDC pipeline (Debezium → Kafka → ClickHouse / Snowflake / BigQuery). 'Real-time analytics' usually means seconds, not milliseconds.
 - 'Star vs snowflake schema?' Star is denormalized (one big dimension table per dimension). Snowflake normalizes further (categories table, brands table). "Star schemas are often preferred because they are simpler for analysts to work with."

Comment: modern stacks usually look like Postgres / MySQL for OLTP + Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift / ClickHouse for OLAP + dbt for transformations. HTAP databases (TiDB, SingleStore, Snowflake's Unistore) try to merge both, with mixed results in practice.

theory.question: Describe column-oriented storage and four optimizations that make it fast on analytical queries: bitmap encoding, vectorized processing, materialized views, and data cubes.
theory.answer:
Headline: a column store transposes the row store — each column gets its own file, with rows in the same order across files. Four optimizations stack on top: bitmap encoding (especially for low-cardinality columns), vectorized SIMD operators over compressed blocks, materialized views that precompute hot aggregates, and data cubes that precompute aggregates across multiple dimensions. This is the engine model behind Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, ClickHouse, and Parquet.

"The idea behind column-oriented storage is simple: don't store all the values from one row together, but store all the values from each column together instead. If each column is stored in a separate file, a query only needs to read and parse those columns that are used in that query, which can save a lot of work."

The layout invariant to mention: "The column-oriented storage layout relies on each column file containing the rows in the same order. Thus, if you need to reassemble an entire row, you can take the 23rd entry from each of the individual column files and put them together to form the 23rd row of the table." Sorting columns independently would break this — sort orders are applied to entire rows even though the data lives in columns.

1. Bitmap encoding (the killer optimization for low-cardinality columns). "Often, the number of distinct values in a column is small compared to the number of rows (for example, a retailer may have billions of sales transactions, but only 100,000 distinct products). We can now take a column with n distinct values and turn it into n separate bitmaps: one bitmap for each distinct value, with one bit for each row." Sparse bitmaps are run-length encoded for further compression. The wins are huge: `WHERE product_sk IN (30, 68, 69)` becomes a bitwise OR of three bitmaps; `WHERE product_sk = 31 AND store_sk = 3` is a bitwise AND. Both are CPU instructions, not B-tree walks.

2. Vectorized processing. "For data warehouse queries that need to scan over millions of rows, a big bottleneck is the bandwidth for getting data from disk into memory. However, that is not the only bottleneck... the query engine can take a chunk of compressed column data that fits comfortably in the CPU's L1 cache and iterate through it in a tight loop (that is, with no function calls)... Operators, such as the bitwise AND and OR described previously, can be designed to operate on such chunks of compressed column data directly. This technique is known as vectorized processing." The mental model: row-at-a-time tuple iteration is replaced by SIMD-style block-at-a-time arithmetic over packed columns. Modern engines run hundreds of millions of rows per second per core because of this.

3. Materialized views. "A materialized view... is a table-like object whose contents are the results of some query. The difference is that a materialized view is an actual copy of the query results, written to disk, whereas a virtual view is just a shortcut for writing queries." "When the underlying data changes, a materialized view needs to be updated, because it is a denormalized copy of the data... such updates make writes more expensive, which is why materialized views are not often used in OLTP databases. In read-heavy data warehouses they can make more sense."

4. Data cubes (a.k.a. OLAP cubes). "A common special case of a materialized view is known as a data cube or OLAP cube. It is a grid of aggregates grouped by different dimensions." Each cell is the aggregate (SUM, COUNT, AVG) of one measure for one combination of dimension values. Hot queries like 'revenue per store per day' become O(1) lookups. The price tag: "A data cube doesn't have the same flexibility as querying the raw data. For example, there is no way of calculating which proportion of sales comes from items that cost more than $100, because the price isn't one of the dimensions." So warehouses keep the raw fact table and use cubes only for the top N hot queries.

Bonus answer on writes: column stores can't do update-in-place easily (would rewrite every column file). The fix is the LSM-tree trick from earlier — buffer writes in an in-memory row store, then bulk-merge them with the column files. "This is essentially what Vertica does."

Follow-up:
 - 'When is column store the wrong choice?' Anything user-facing, point-lookup, transactional. Don't put your shopping cart in ClickHouse.
 - 'How do you keep the cube fresh?' Incrementally — Snowflake and ClickHouse have `SUMMING`/aggregating materialized views that update on write. Or refresh on a schedule for hourly dashboards.
 - 'Why is Parquet so popular?' Same column-store ideas, but as a portable file format — column groups, dictionary encoding (bitmap-style), delta encoding for timestamps. Lets every engine (Spark, Trino, DuckDB, Snowflake) read the same files.

Comment: ClickHouse's `LowCardinality(String)` is bitmap encoding made into a column type; `SummingMergeTree` and `AggregatingMergeTree` are the cube engine. DuckDB makes the whole stack runnable on a laptop with the same column-store, vectorized-execution architecture.

practice.task: [PostgreSQL vs ClickHouse] You have an e-commerce orders system. Pick the right database (or combination) for the workload below and write the CREATE TABLE plus indexes for each side.
practice.question:
An e-commerce platform has two very different query patterns hitting the same logical 'orders' data.

Workload A — operational queries:
```
  -- Fetch one order for the order-details page
  SELECT id, customer_id, status, total_amount, created_at
  FROM   orders
  WHERE  id = $1;

  -- Fetch a customer's recent orders for the account page
  SELECT id, status, total_amount, created_at
  FROM   orders
  WHERE  customer_id = $1
  ORDER BY created_at DESC
  LIMIT  20;
```

Workload A rates:
  - 30 000 reads per second
  - 5 000 writes per second (new orders, status updates)
  - p99 latency target: under 50 ms
  - dataset: ~500 GB, growing 1 GB / day

Workload B — analytics queries:
```
  -- Monthly revenue per product category, last 12 months
  SELECT date_trunc('month', o.created_at)   AS month,
         p.category,
         SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price)     AS revenue,
         COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id)        AS unique_buyers
  FROM   orders         o
  JOIN   order_items    oi ON oi.order_id   = o.id
  JOIN   products       p  ON p.id          = oi.product_id
  WHERE  o.created_at >= now() - interval '12 months'
  GROUP  BY date_trunc('month', o.created_at), p.category
  ORDER  BY month, revenue DESC;

  -- Daily checkout funnel for last 90 days
  SELECT date_trunc('day', created_at) AS day,
         status,
         COUNT(*)                       AS orders
  FROM   orders
  WHERE  created_at >= now() - interval '90 days'
  GROUP  BY date_trunc('day', created_at), status;
```

Workload B rates:
  - 50 queries per second (BI dashboards, internal tools)
  - bulk ingest of 1M historical events / day from the OLTP store
  - p99 latency target: under 5 s
  - dataset: 5 TB and growing

Answer two questions:
  1. Which database engine should run each workload, and why?
  2. Provide the CREATE TABLE + index DDL for each side. Use realistic types and explain why each index is there.
practice.answer:
Run two engines side by side. PostgreSQL owns OLTP (workload A). ClickHouse owns OLAP (workload B). Debezium streams CDC events into Kafka, a small Kafka-to-ClickHouse pipeline lands them in a column-oriented analytical schema.

```
-- =============== PostgreSQL (OLTP) ===============
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id            BIGSERIAL     PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id   BIGINT        NOT NULL,
  status        TEXT          NOT NULL,
  total_amount  NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ   NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ   NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
-- B-tree primary key already covers `WHERE id = $1`.
-- Composite (customer_id, created_at DESC) covers account-page reads.
CREATE INDEX orders_customer_recent_idx ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC);
-- Partial index keeps the WIP-orders index small for the warehouse picking system.
CREATE INDEX orders_open_idx ON orders (status, created_at)
  WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing');

CREATE TABLE order_items (
  order_id    BIGINT  NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id),
  product_id  BIGINT  NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id),
  quantity    INT     NOT NULL,
  unit_price  NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);

-- =============== ClickHouse (OLAP) ===============
-- Star schema: one wide fact table + dimension tables.
CREATE TABLE fact_orders (
  event_date    Date,
  event_time    DateTime,
  order_id      UInt64,
  customer_id   UInt64,
  product_id    UInt32,
  category_id   LowCardinality(String),     -- enables bitmap-like compression
  status        LowCardinality(String),
  country_code  LowCardinality(FixedString(2)),
  quantity      UInt32,
  unit_price    Decimal(10,2),
  revenue       Decimal(12,2) MATERIALIZED quantity * unit_price
)
ENGINE = MergeTree                          -- column-oriented LSM-style storage
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_date)
ORDER BY (event_date, category_id, customer_id)  -- sort key drives compression + skip indexes
SETTINGS index_granularity = 8192;

-- Materialized view that maintains the monthly-by-category aggregate as data lands.
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW fact_orders_monthly_by_category
ENGINE = SummingMergeTree
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(month)
ORDER BY (month, category_id)
AS
SELECT toStartOfMonth(event_date) AS month,
       category_id,
       sum(revenue)              AS revenue,
       uniqState(customer_id)    AS unique_buyers_state
FROM   fact_orders
GROUP  BY month, category_id;
```

Why this is the canonical answer:
  - PostgreSQL B-tree indexes win on the OLTP path: small key-driven reads, predictable latency, locks attached directly to the B-tree for transactional `UPDATE orders SET status = ...`.
  - ClickHouse stores each column in its own file, applies bitmap-style encoding to `LowCardinality` columns (status, category, country), and runs `sum()` and bitwise AND/OR filters in vectorized SIMD loops over compressed blocks — the four optimizations from the chapter (column storage + bitmap encoding + vectorized processing + materialized views).
  - Partitioning by month and sorting by `(event_date, category_id)` means the 'last 12 months' BI query only scans 12 partitions, and the first sort key compresses to almost nothing thanks to run-length encoding of `category_id` within each day.
  - The `SummingMergeTree` materialized view auto-incrementally maintains the monthly aggregate so the dashboard's main chart is an O(1) read per month per category.

Operational notes (what production actually looks like):
  - Synchronize via Debezium → Kafka → ClickHouse Kafka engine, with the message key = `orders.id` so updates land on the correct partition.
  - Replication lag (Postgres → ClickHouse) typically settles under 10 s. The product page must always read from Postgres; only the analytics layer reads ClickHouse.
  - Backfills run with `INSERT INTO fact_orders SELECT ... FROM postgres_fdw_orders` for the historical 5 TB; new data comes in incrementally.
  - Monitor: ClickHouse `system.parts` (merge backlog), Postgres `pg_stat_user_indexes` (index bloat), Kafka consumer lag.

Tradeoffs worth acknowledging:
  - Two systems = two ops surfaces. Cost is justified only past the point where the analytics queries hurt the OLTP path.
  - Eventual consistency between the two: the order-details page must never read from ClickHouse, or the customer will see stale order status.
  - ClickHouse cannot do row-level transactions; the OLTP system is the source of truth.

practice.task: [PostgreSQL] Choose the right secondary indexes for a chat messages table given three different read patterns.
practice.question:
A chat product stores messages in PostgreSQL. The table is small per row but grows quickly — currently 2 billion rows, ~1 TB on disk.

```
CREATE TABLE messages (
  id          BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  channel_id  BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  author_id   BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  body        TEXT         NOT NULL,
  edited_at   TIMESTAMPTZ,
  deleted_at  TIMESTAMPTZ
);
```

Three read patterns hit this table:
  Q1 — channel timeline (extremely hot, ~100k qps):
```
      SELECT id, author_id, created_at, body
      FROM   messages
      WHERE  channel_id = $1
        AND  deleted_at IS NULL
      ORDER  BY created_at DESC
      LIMIT  50;
```

  Q2 — author search inside a channel (~500 qps):
```
      SELECT id, created_at, body
      FROM   messages
      WHERE  channel_id = $1 AND author_id = $2
      ORDER  BY created_at DESC
      LIMIT  50;
```

  Q3 — keyword search across a channel (~200 qps):
```
      SELECT id, author_id, created_at, ts_headline('english', body, q) AS snippet
      FROM   messages, plainto_tsquery('english', $2) q
      WHERE  channel_id = $1
        AND  to_tsvector('english', body) @@ q
      ORDER  BY ts_rank(to_tsvector('english', body), q) DESC
      LIMIT  20;
```

Write the CREATE INDEX statements that make all three queries fast. For each index, explain in one sentence which column ordering you picked and why.
practice.answer:
```
-- Q1: channel timeline, hottest query.
-- Composite B-tree with partial index excludes soft-deleted rows entirely.
CREATE INDEX messages_channel_timeline_idx
  ON messages (channel_id, created_at DESC)
  INCLUDE (author_id, body)        -- covering index: row data is satisfied from the index alone
  WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;

-- Q2: author within a channel — composite key avoids two-step lookup.
CREATE INDEX messages_channel_author_idx
  ON messages (channel_id, author_id, created_at DESC)
  WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;

-- Q3: keyword search — generalized inverted index (GIN) on a tsvector.
-- This is the PostgreSQL analogue of a Lucene postings list.
CREATE INDEX messages_fts_idx
  ON messages
  USING GIN (to_tsvector('english', body))
  WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;

-- For very large tables, partition by channel_id (hash) or by month (range) before adding indexes
-- so each B-tree stays under ~50 GB. Then each index above becomes one per partition.
```

Why each choice is the right shape:
  1. The Q1 index leads on the equality column (`channel_id`) and has the range/sort column second (`created_at DESC`) — that is the standard composite-index rule (equality, then range, then sort).
     The chapter's quote applies: a covering index 'stores some of a table's columns within the index. This allows some queries to be answered by using the index alone.' INCLUDE (author_id, body) gives Postgres an index-only scan and skips the heap fetch entirely for the timeline page.
  2. The partial predicate `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` keeps deleted messages out of the index — smaller index, less write amplification on UPDATE messages SET deleted_at = now(), and the planner does not need an extra IS NULL filter.
  3. Q2 needs `(channel_id, author_id)` as its leading prefix; with the Q1 index alone the planner would have to filter author_id row by row.
  4. Q3 is a full-text search. PostgreSQL's GIN index over `to_tsvector(body)` is conceptually the same as Lucene's term → postings-list structure described in the chapter: each token maps to a list of row IDs containing it. Without this index, every keyword query becomes a full table scan.

Write cost — the tradeoff worth calling out:
  - Each INSERT updates four indexes (PK + Q1 + Q2 + Q3). The GIN index is the slowest because it tokenizes the body. For 100k qps reads with only ~5k inserts/sec, this is the correct trade-off.
  - The chapter's note applies: 'well-chosen indexes speed up read queries, but every index slows down writes.' Confirm by monitoring `pg_stat_statements` and writeback throughput before adding more indexes.

practice.task: [PostgreSQL] A user-profile API is slow. Diagnose the secondary-index bug in the schema below and provide the fix.
practice.question:
The existing schema and indexes:

```
CREATE TABLE users (
  id           BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  email        TEXT         UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  country      TEXT         NOT NULL,
  signup_date  DATE         NOT NULL,
  last_login   TIMESTAMPTZ
);

CREATE INDEX users_signup_country_idx ON users (signup_date, country);
```

The hot query:
```
  SELECT id, email, last_login
  FROM   users
  WHERE  country = 'DE'
    AND  signup_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
  ORDER  BY signup_date DESC
  LIMIT  100;
```

`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` shows a Bitmap Heap Scan reading 8 million rows when only ~300 000 exist for Germany. The table has 200 million rows total. Identify why the existing index is the wrong shape and provide a corrected `CREATE INDEX` plus a brief rationale.
practice.answer:
Problem (the headline answer):
The existing index is `(signup_date, country)`. The query filters by `country = 'DE'` (equality) and then `signup_date BETWEEN ...` (range). With this column order, PostgreSQL must walk the index range across every signup_date in 2024 and filter rows where `country = 'DE'` afterwards — exactly the scenario the book describes for the phone-book analogy:

  'Due to the sort order, the index can be used to find all the people with a particular last name, or all the people with a particular lastname-firstname combination. However, the index is useless if you want to find all the people with a particular first name.'

Here `signup_date` is acting like 'last name'. We want to start with country (the high-selectivity equality predicate) and then filter the date range inside that country.

Fix:
```
DROP INDEX users_signup_country_idx;

CREATE INDEX users_country_signup_idx
  ON users (country, signup_date DESC)
  INCLUDE (email, last_login);
```

What changes:
  - The leading column is now the equality predicate (`country`), so Postgres seeks directly to country = 'DE' and only walks the ~300 000 rows for Germany.
  - The secondary column is the range / sort column (`signup_date DESC`), so the ORDER BY is satisfied by reading the index in order — no separate Sort node.
  - INCLUDE columns make this a covering index: the LIMIT 100 page is returned without touching the heap at all (an index-only scan, in PostgreSQL terms).

Verification:
```
  EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
  SELECT id, email, last_login FROM users
  WHERE country = 'DE' AND signup_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
  ORDER BY signup_date DESC LIMIT 100;
  -- Before: Bitmap Heap Scan + Sort, ~2 s, ~8M rows scanned
  -- After:  Index Only Scan, ~3 ms, ~100 rows scanned
```

The one-line takeaway: composite indexes go equality first, range / sort columns last, and use INCLUDE to make the index cover the SELECT list whenever the page is hot.

practice.task: [MySQL / InnoDB] An orders table is suffering massive write amplification and table bloat. Diagnose the clustered-index bug and write the migration that fixes it.
practice.question:
An e-commerce backend stores orders in MySQL/InnoDB. The team picked UUIDv4 as the primary key three months ago to make IDs unguessable and to avoid coordinating across services.

```
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id            BINARY(16)    NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,   -- stores a UUIDv4
  customer_id   BIGINT        NOT NULL,
  status        VARCHAR(32)   NOT NULL,
  total_amount  DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
  created_at    DATETIME(6)   NOT NULL,
  notes         VARCHAR(500)
) ENGINE = InnoDB;

CREATE INDEX orders_customer_recent_idx ON orders (customer_id, created_at);
CREATE INDEX orders_status_idx           ON orders (status);
```

Application insert path (Python, simplified):
```
  order_id = uuid.uuid4().bytes
  cursor.execute(
    "INSERT INTO orders (id, customer_id, status, total_amount, created_at) "
    "VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s)",
    (order_id, customer_id, 'pending', amount, datetime.utcnow())
  )
```

Symptoms after three months in production (5 000 inserts/sec growing to 8 000/sec):
  - Table is ~2 TB on disk, but the raw data is only ~700 GB → roughly 3× bloat.
  - `SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS` reports thousands of page splits per minute under steady load.
  - p99 insert latency drifted from ~2 ms to ~35 ms; disk IOPS roughly doubled.
  - The buffer-pool hit rate fell from 99.5 % to 92 %.
  - Both secondary indexes are noticeably larger than expected.

Deliver:
  1. A short root-cause explanation that connects the symptoms back to a specific property of InnoDB's storage engine.
  2. The schema change that fixes the underlying problem.
  3. A migration plan that gets there with no downtime.
practice.answer:
Root cause (the headline answer):
InnoDB stores every table as a B+ tree keyed by the primary key — the primary key is the clustered index, exactly as the chapter notes: 'In MySQL's InnoDB storage engine, the primary key of a table is always a clustered index, and secondary indexes refer to the primary key.' UUIDv4 is uniformly random, so each new insert lands on a random leaf page of that B+ tree.

When the random leaf has no free space, the B-tree algorithm runs the path described in the chapter: 'If there isn't enough free space in the page to accommodate the new key, it is split into two half-full pages, and the parent page is updated to account for the new subdivision of key ranges.' That single insert turns into 'rewrite leaf page A + rewrite leaf page B + rewrite the parent page', and the chapter warns that 'a B-tree index must write every piece of data at least twice: once to the write-ahead log, and once to the tree page itself (and perhaps again as pages are split).'

Three concrete consequences of UUIDv4 → random PK → constant page splits:
  1. Table bloat. Every split leaves two half-empty 16 KB pages. "B-tree storage engines leave some disk space unused due to fragmentation: when a page is split or when a row cannot fit into an existing page, some space in a page remains unused." 700 GB of raw data sits in ~2 TB of half-empty pages.
  2. Buffer-pool thrashing. A random insert touches a random leaf page — almost never the one that is hot from the previous insert. Each insert pulls a cold page into the buffer pool, so reads compete with inserts for cache space and the hit rate collapses.
  3. Fat secondary indexes. InnoDB secondary indexes store the PK as the row reference. With BIGINT PKs that is 8 bytes per entry; with BINARY(16) UUIDs it is 16 bytes plus alignment overhead, so every secondary index is ~2× larger and re-reads it more often miss the buffer pool. The write-amplification cost from earlier in the chapter compounds: every insert writes to the clustered index + every secondary index + the WAL.

A monotonic PK eliminates all of this. Inserts land at the right edge of the B+ tree, the rightmost leaf fills sequentially, and splits happen on a predictable schedule with no random-page I/O.

Fix — schema change:

```
ALTER TABLE orders
  ADD COLUMN order_id_v7 BINARY(16) NOT NULL DEFAULT (UUID_TO_BIN(UUID(), 1)) AFTER id,
  ADD UNIQUE KEY uq_order_id_v7 (order_id_v7);
```

UUIDv7 (RFC 9562) keeps the 16-byte width and the global-uniqueness property but encodes the Unix millisecond timestamp in the high bits, so values are monotonic for inserts in the same millisecond bucket. Equivalent drop-in replacements: ULID (also time-prefixed), Snowflake (BIGINT, encodes timestamp + worker + sequence), or — if you don't need globally-unique IDs — plain BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT. Postgres 18 ships `uuidv7()` built in; MySQL 8 needs a helper function or generation in the app layer.

No-downtime migration plan:
  1. Dual-write phase. Deploy code that fills both `id` (UUIDv4) and `order_id_v7` on every insert. Existing rows back-fill via a small batched UPDATE job (LIMIT 1000 per statement, rate-limited so it doesn't starve replicas).
  2. Read switch. Add `order_id_v7` to every read path; route external API responses to it; keep the old `id` valid for the cutover window.
  3. PK swap (the only careful step). InnoDB cannot change the PK without rebuilding the clustered index, so use `ALTER TABLE orders DROP PRIMARY KEY, ADD PRIMARY KEY (order_id_v7), ALGORITHM=COPY, LOCK=SHARED;` or use a tool that copies the table online: `pt-online-schema-change` (Percona) or `gh-ost` (GitHub). Both keep writes flowing by chunk-copying rows into a shadow table and replaying the binlog against it before the final atomic rename.
  4. Drop the old column. After a safe observation window (at least a week, ideally a backup cycle), `ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN id;` This is the step that actually reclaims the bloated disk space — pt-online-schema-change does that automatically because it creates a fresh shadow table.
  5. Verify. After the rebuild, the table is roughly the raw-data size again (~700 GB), buffer-pool hit rate recovers, page-split counter goes back to near zero, and p99 insert latency drops back to the original 2-3 ms.

Tradeoffs to acknowledge:
  - `pt-online-schema-change` and `gh-ost` run a full table copy; on a 2 TB table that's hours of binlog replay and disk pressure. Schedule during off-peak and watch replica lag.
  - UUIDv7 still leaks a coarse insertion timestamp (millisecond resolution). If that's a privacy concern, use Snowflake with a private worker ID or random tie-breaker bits.
  - If foreign keys reference `orders.id` from many other tables, the migration needs to propagate the new column to every reference before the PK swap — that's usually the longest part of the project.

Follow-up:
  - 'What if we cannot change the PK at all?' Keep UUIDv4 PK but adopt the InnoDB workaround: a hidden 64-bit `_rowid_` AUTO_INCREMENT clustered index, with a unique secondary index on the UUID. Inserts then hit the rowid B-tree sequentially. Trade: every PK lookup is two B-tree walks.
  - 'How does PostgreSQL behave differently?' Postgres uses a heap table (not a clustered index), so the bloat from random PKs is much smaller — but the random-page-I/O effect on the unique B-tree index still exists. `uuid` columns there commonly add ~10-15 % index bloat, not 3×.
  - 'How do you detect this in monitoring?' Track `Innodb_page_splits` (or `innodb_metrics.index_page_splits`), the ratio of `Innodb_buffer_pool_pages_dirty` to total, and table bloat via `information_schema.tables.data_free`.

Comment: this exact bug has hit Shopify, GitHub, and Discord at scale and is the canonical reason MySQL shops adopt Snowflake / ULID / UUIDv7. Marco Slot's blog post 'Do you really need UUIDs/ULIDs?' and Percona's 'Storing UUID Values in MySQL Tables' (https://www.percona.com/blog/store-uuid-optimized-way/) are the standard references.

practice.task: [ClickHouse] Demonstrate column-oriented + bitmap-style storage by writing the schema and queries for a clickstream analytics table that needs `country IN (...)`, `device_type = ...`, and daily aggregations to all run in well under a second over 1 billion rows.
practice.question:
A product analytics service ingests web events. Every event has roughly 30 columns; analysts typically only touch 4-6 of them in any one query.

Sample event:
```
  { event_time, user_id, session_id, country, device_type, os, browser, referrer,
    utm_source, utm_campaign, page_path, page_load_ms, ... }
```

Query patterns (run on ClickHouse, all over 1 billion rows):
  Q1 — DAU per country, last 30 days, for desktop + mobile only:
```
        SELECT toDate(event_time) AS day, country, uniq(user_id) AS dau
        FROM   events
        WHERE  event_time >= now() - INTERVAL 30 DAY
          AND  country IN ('DE','US','GB','FR','BR','IN','JP')
          AND  device_type IN ('desktop','mobile')
        GROUP  BY day, country
        ORDER  BY day, dau DESC;
```

  Q2 — Browser distribution for a single campaign:
```
        SELECT browser, count() AS hits
        FROM   events
        WHERE  utm_campaign = 'spring_sale_2026'
          AND  event_time >= '2026-03-01'
        GROUP  BY browser
        ORDER  BY hits DESC
        LIMIT  10;
```

Write the CREATE TABLE for `events` so that:
  - Only the columns the queries reference are read from disk.
  - The low-cardinality string columns get the bitmap-like compression the book describes.
  - The 30-day filter does not require scanning the whole table.

Explain which optimizations from chapter 3 each piece of the DDL maps to.
practice.answer:
```
CREATE TABLE events (
  event_time       DateTime CODEC(DoubleDelta, ZSTD(3)),    -- timestamps compress extremely well with delta encoding
  event_date       Date DEFAULT toDate(event_time),
  user_id          UInt64,
  session_id       UUID,
  -- Low-cardinality columns: ~200 countries, ~5 device types, etc.
  country          LowCardinality(FixedString(2)),
  device_type      LowCardinality(String),
  os               LowCardinality(String),
  browser          LowCardinality(String),
  referrer         String,
  utm_source       LowCardinality(String),
  utm_campaign     LowCardinality(String),
  page_path        String,
  page_load_ms     UInt32
)
ENGINE = MergeTree
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_date)                     -- physical pruning by month
ORDER BY (event_date, country, device_type, user_id)  -- sort key drives compression + skip indexes
SETTINGS index_granularity = 8192;

-- Optional secondary indexes for very selective filters.
-- Bloom filter on utm_campaign accelerates Q2 by skipping granules that cannot contain the campaign.
ALTER TABLE events
  ADD INDEX idx_utm_campaign utm_campaign TYPE bloom_filter GRANULARITY 4;

ALTER TABLE events
  ADD INDEX idx_country country TYPE set(0) GRANULARITY 8;
```

Mapping each piece of the DDL back to a chapter-3 optimization:

1. Column-oriented storage.
   ClickHouse's MergeTree engine writes each column into its own file inside each part. Q1 only reads `event_time, country, device_type, user_id` — four out of thirty columns — exactly as the chapter describes: 'a query only needs to read and parse those columns that are used in that query.'

2. Bitmap encoding via LowCardinality.
   `LowCardinality(String)` stores the column as a dictionary of distinct values plus a small integer index per row. This is the practical form of the chapter's bitmap encoding: 'a column with n distinct values [becomes] n separate bitmaps: one bitmap for each distinct value, with one bit for each row.' A WHERE clause like `country IN ('DE','US','GB')` becomes a bitwise OR over three tiny bit-sets. With 200 distinct countries, a billion-row country column compresses to a handful of MB.

3. Vectorized processing.
   The query engine reads compressed blocks of (event_date, country) directly into L1 cache and applies the filters as SIMD bitwise operators. The chapter's exact phrasing: 'Operators, such as the bitwise AND and OR described previously, can be designed to operate on such chunks of compressed column data directly. This technique is known as vectorized processing.'

4. Sort key as 'first-sort-column compresses best'.
   The ORDER BY clause in the DDL is the storage sort order, not the query sort order. By leading with `event_date`, all rows for the same day are physically adjacent — run-length encoding on event_date collapses billions of rows to a few thousand RLE entries. The chapter: 'If the primary sort column does not have many distinct values, then after sorting, it will have long sequences where the same value is repeated many times in a row. A simple run-length encoding... could compress that column down to a few kilobytes — even if the table has billions of rows.'

5. Partitioning replaces 'pages on disk' with monthly chunks.
   `PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_date)` means the 'last 30 days' filter only opens at most 2 monthly parts instead of scanning the whole table.

6. LSM-style writes.
   MergeTree inserts go to in-memory parts then flush to immutable sorted files and merge in the background, mirroring exactly what the chapter says about column-store writes: 'All writes first go to an in-memory store, where they are added to a sorted structure and prepared for writing to disk... When enough writes have accumulated, they are merged with the column files on disk and written to new files in bulk. This is essentially what Vertica does.'

A SummingMergeTree materialized view can take it one step further by precomputing the data cube the chapter describes:

```
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW events_daily_country_device
ENGINE = SummingMergeTree
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(day)
ORDER BY (day, country, device_type)
AS
SELECT toDate(event_time) AS day,
       country,
       device_type,
       uniqState(user_id) AS dau_state,
       count()           AS hits
FROM   events
GROUP  BY day, country, device_type;
```

Q1 reads a few hundred pre-aggregated rows from this view instead of scanning billions of raw events — at the cost of denormalized storage and the standard data-cube limitation noted in the chapter ('there is no way of calculating which proportion of sales comes from items that cost more than $100, because the price isn't one of the dimensions').
